topic: Australia road scaffold validation — post-EXP-054 boilerplate + quantitative imprecisions
change_summary: Since c2 (2026-04-08T21:00), WIN-069 has grown: detail_evidence 2810→4786 (+70%) and detail_verdict_text 396→1351 (+241%). Main additions: (a) selection-bias paragraph added in commit 121d487 addressing c2 hole #3; (b) standardized post-hoc rebuttal boilerplate appended to detail_verdict_text in EXP-054 integration (commit 58dbd15, 2026-04-10). c2 holes #1 (imprecise 10,000× claim) was partially addressed by adding the 10,000×–250,000× range, but the comparison is still numerically inconsistent with millimeter-level GNSS. c2 hole #2 (terrain-based model defense) was addressed rhetorically but not with a cited alternative. New issue surfaced by the EXP-054 boilerplate: internal tag↔text contradiction — detail_verdict_text now asserts the numerical value 'is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters,' but code_analysis.derives_from_dome is still tagged false.
our_argument_summary: Our review argues: (1) Australian road excess over geodesic is a geographic/terrain fact, not a topology signature; (2) the 6.2% RMSE is orders of magnitude worse than standard geodesy (~10,000×–250,000× depending on comparator); (3) the AGD66→GDA94 datum shift is a routine regional→geocentric transition identical to shifts on every continent; (4) V13 parameters (a=π×R_Earth=20,015 km, equatorial ring=14,105 km) are calibrated from globe distances, making validation circular; (5) the selected 12 roads/9 cross-links are an undisclosed sample from thousands of possible pairs, raising selection-bias concerns. Verdict: Misleading. Post-EXP-054 boilerplate reinforces the post-hoc/circular critique in detail_verdict_text.

relevant_files: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt (V12 coordinate system — V13 added after extraction)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: V13 two-zone topology: disc radius a = 20,015 km ≈ π×R_Earth, equatorial ring at 14,105 km, refractive profile n(r) containing H(r) = 8,537·exp(−r/8,619) km. Predicted inter-city distances are computed from this geometry and compared against globe-measured road distances to yield 6.2% cross-equatorial RMSE and 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error. No monitor.py automation for this WIN — this is a one-time static claim, not a live prediction.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: No monitoring automation. The 6.2% RMSE is a static claim on the dome site; no API fetch or live revalidation. a and ring radius are hard-coded to globe-derived values (π×6371 and a fit to WGS84 distances).
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: V13 has multiple free parameters (H₀, λ, equatorial ring radius, angular scaling factor ~0.9941) that can be retuned. Nothing prevents the 6.2% from being improved by further parameter fitting without any new dome physics.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None found. The review correctly identifies this as unmonitored and parameter-calibrated.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No monitor.py entry for this WIN in the V50.6 raw-text snapshot (raw-text/monitor.py predates V51.0 WINs 068/069). The 6.2% figure is published as a static claim on the dome's page, with no live fetch or automated recomputation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics has no 'road-distance-vs-geodesic' predictive framework; the excess is treated as a topography/network-engineering question. The dome model invents a topology-based explanation rather than relabeling a standard physics explanation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: All Australian road distances, WGS84 inter-city distances, Nullarbor segment lengths, and the AGD66→GDA94 datum history are published well before V13. The dome adopted these known quantities and labels matching them a 'confirmed prediction.'
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The V13 topology is structurally dome-specific, BUT the key numerical inputs (disc radius a = π×R_Earth, equatorial ring radius 14,105 km, H(r) scale) are fit to globe-calibrated measurements. The 'prediction' is circular — dome parameters are set to reproduce globe distances, then the approximate reproduction is claimed as confirmation. Note: this tag is in tension with the new EXP-054 detail_verdict_text boilerplate, which reads 'the numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters' — see hole #1 below. WIN-057 is tagged derives_from_dome=true for the same V13 topology (structural view); WIN-069 is tagged false (calibration view). Either both should be true and we switch to a 'derived structure, calibrated parameters' framing, or the EXP-054 boilerplate needs per-WIN wording for this case.

holes_found.description: INTERNAL TAG↔TEXT CONTRADICTION introduced by EXP-054 boilerplate (commit 58dbd15, 2026-04-10). detail_verdict_text now asserts: 'The numerical value is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters (disc radius, firmament height, chosen symmetry axis, or similar), and the "confirmation" consists of matching that derivation to a globe-measured value — which, because the dome parameters were themselves fit to globe data (see Kill-Shot #1 and Part 2b), is circular.' This directly asserts the dome DOES derive the value from dome geometry. However, code_analysis.derives_from_dome is still tagged false. A careful dome defender reading both the tag and the verdict paragraph finds us in Part 2b-style self-contradiction: our tagged data says 'not derived from dome' (Part 2b counts), but our verdict prose says 'derived from dome parameters.' Parallel inconsistency with WIN-057 (derives_from_dome=true for the same V13 topology) makes this worse.
holes_found.recommendation: Flip derives_from_dome to true on WIN-069, matching WIN-057 and the EXP-054 verdict boilerplate — the V13 structure IS dome-derived even if parameters are calibrated. Update derives_from_dome_evidence to note 'V13 topology + refractive profile are dome-derived; numerical parameters are fit to globe data; the combination is circular retrodiction.' This brings tag and verdict text into agreement and also resolves the WIN-057/WIN-069 cross-WIN inconsistency flagged in c2. Regenerate any Part 2b aggregate counts that depend on derives_from_dome to match. Alternatively (weaker fix) reword the EXP-054 boilerplate for WIN-069 to say 'the numerical value is computed from a dome-specific topology whose parameters were themselves calibrated to globe data' — preserving the tag but removing the 'derives from dome's own parameters' claim.

holes_found.description: QUANTITATIVE IMPRECISION in '10,000× vs. millimeter-level GNSS positioning, up to 250,000× vs. sub-mm VLBI geodesy.' Direct computation: 6.2% RMSE applied to a 4,000 km transcontinental route = 248 km absolute error. Millimeter-level GNSS positioning on a 4,000 km baseline gives ~1 mm / 4,000,000,000 mm ≈ 2.5×10⁻¹⁰ relative accuracy (or, if restated as absolute positioning, ~1 mm on a single point). The honest ratio vs mm-GNSS is ~10⁸ (hundreds of millions), not 10,000×. The 10,000× figure actually corresponds to comparing 6.2% to ~meter-level positioning (sub-meter GPS on 4,000 km ≈ 2.5×10⁻⁷ → ratio 248,000× — close to the 250,000× sub-mm VLBI figure, NOT to 10,000× mm-GNSS). The upper and lower endpoints of the stated range are labelled with the wrong comparators. A dome-defender AI, pasted this paragraph, will instantly flag the mismatch: 'Your review's own numbers don't cohere — mm-level and sub-mm differ by a factor of ~5×, yet you claim a 25× spread.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) simplify to a single, correctly-labelled ratio — e.g., 'the dome's 6.2% RMSE is roughly 2.5×10⁵× worse than modern sub-meter distance accuracy (1 m on ~4,000 km transcontinental baselines), and ~10⁸× worse than millimeter-precision geodetic positioning' — or (b) relabel the endpoints: '10,000× vs. consumer/meter-scale GPS, up to 250,000× vs. geodetic-grade sub-meter distance accuracy, and far more vs. VLBI baseline determination.' The numerical claim in the top-level 'finding' field ('10,000× worse than geodetic standards') and in both TLDRs should be updated in lockstep to whichever framing is chosen. The current text offers a dome defender an open door to dispute our central quantitative comparison.

holes_found.description: TLDR-DETAIL ANCHOR GAP. tldr_evidence asserts 'The Nullarbor Plain straight road deviates only 0.4% from the geodesic' and tldr_verdict repeats 'with only 0.4% deviation.' But the expanded detail_evidence does NOT contain any '0.4%' figure — it says 'ratio approaches 1.00 on its dead-straight central segment (~146 km)' and '~1.07 for the full Norseman-Ceduna crossing.' The 0.4% number thus has no anchor in the expanded evidence a reader can click through to. A careful critic will ask 'where does 0.4% come from?' and find nothing. Physically, a truly straight 146-km road on the WGS84 ellipsoid has a road-to-geodesic ratio differing from 1 by well under 0.01% (sub-meter ellipsoid deviation over 146 km), so '0.4%' is actually a conservative loose claim, but the gap between TLDR specificity and detail vagueness is itself a rhetorical hole.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a single explicit sentence to detail_evidence stating the 0.4% figure and its source, e.g., 'The 90-Mile Straight (146.6 km, Balladonia–Caiguna) deviates from its great-circle geodesic by less than 0.4% — effectively zero for a physically straight road on the ellipsoid'; or (b) drop the specific 0.4% from both TLDRs and match the detail's 'approaches 1.00' wording. Option (a) is stronger — the 0.4% upper bound IS actually well-supported and is a useful discrimination point against dome topology, which would predict a non-trivial latitude-dependent excess even on flat terrain.

holes_found.description: UNSOURCED COUNTER-MODEL in the strongest-defense paragraph. We assert: 'terrain-based routing models (using digital elevation and road network data) explain road excess as a function of topographic roughness.' We do not cite any specific terrain-based model, detour-index study, or routing-engine benchmark. A defender's strongest rebuttal lands squarely here: 'Show us a terrain-based model with published RMSE on our 12-road sample — without that, V13's 6.2% is the only quantitative attempt and you are waving at an imagined alternative.' See also advocate_mode below.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one concrete citation to the terrain/detour literature. Two serviceable options: (1) detour-index work such as Huber & Rust (2016, R Journal 'circuity' functions) documenting typical road-network circuity of 1.05–1.20 as a function of terrain, achieving far better than 6.2% RMSE for mean-ratio prediction; (2) an OSRM or Valhalla benchmark showing that, given only origin/destination coordinates and public elevation/network data, routing engines predict observed road distances to sub-1% error. Even a single 1-sentence citation would close this defense.

holes_found.description: STALE NUMERICAL CLAIM IN 'finding' FIELD. The summary-table finding reads: '6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than geodetic standards; AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology.' The 10,000× figure here is the same loosely-labelled number discussed in hole #2 — but in the 'finding' field there is no range, no qualification about comparator, and no disclaimer. Because summary rows are the first (and often only) thing a casual reader encounters, a factually-shaky number here is amplified. Relatedly, the 10.2% same-hemisphere mean error (a larger error than the cross-equatorial 6.2%) is never mentioned in the finding — yet it is arguably MORE damaging to the dome's claim than the 6.2% figure, because V13 predicts excess on cross-equatorial (long) routes, so the same-hemisphere error being HIGHER than the cross-equatorial one is a dome-internal anomaly we should weaponize.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the finding to (a) replace '10,000× worse than geodetic standards' with a defensible framing (e.g., 'orders of magnitude worse than sub-meter geodesy') and (b) lead with the same-hemisphere > cross-equatorial anomaly: 'V13 predicts long cross-equatorial routes should diverge most, yet same-hemisphere mean error (10.2%) exceeds cross-equatorial RMSE (6.2%) — the opposite of what the topology claims.'

holes_found.description: 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' (selection-bias paragraph) numerically understates. Australia's drivable road network has on the order of 10⁵–10⁶ significant road segments and on the order of 10¹⁰ pair combinations. 'Thousands' is correct in the sense that the dome sampled 12 from a pool >>12, but it arbitrarily chooses a small-sounding denominator that a defender can challenge ('actually we sampled 12 from dozens of major transcontinental routes, not thousands of random pairs').
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'thousands of possible Australian road pairs' to 'the full set of inter-city road pairs spanning Australia' or to a defensible specific count (e.g., 'the ~50 pairs among Australia's 15 largest cities'). Avoids giving the defender a number to haggle with.

stronger_arguments.description: The SAME-HEMISPHERE > CROSS-EQUATORIAL anomaly is a dome-internal self-contradiction worth promoting. The dome's V13 narrative: the ovoid cavity expansion manifests most on long trans-equatorial routes; local southern-hemisphere controls should stay near-geodesic. The data reported: cross-equatorial RMSE = 6.2%, same-hemisphere mean error = 10.2%. If V13 were real, same-hemisphere error should be LESS than cross-equatorial. It is MORE. This is currently buried in a paragraph; it should be the headline anti-V13 argument.
sources: Dome V13 page Tier-1 Steelman Evidence Ladder (6.2% / 10.2% figures as quoted on john09289.github.io)

stronger_arguments.description: Detour-index / road-circuity literature (Huber & Rust 2016; also Giacomin & Levinson 2015 'Road network circuity in metropolitan areas') documents that detour index ≈ 1.1–1.2 globally, varying with terrain and network density. A naive 'predicted road distance = geodesic × 1.12' rule (mean detour index for non-mountainous large continents) applied to 12 Australian routes would yield RMSE comparable to or better than V13's 6.2%, with ONE free parameter vs V13's ≥4 parameters.
sources: Huber & Rust 2016, R Journal, 'detour' package documentation; Giacomin & Levinson 2015, Transportation Planning and Technology

stronger_arguments.description: The AGD66→GDA94 counter-argument can be sharpened: the ~200 m shift is QUANTITATIVELY PREDICTED by globe geometry (offset of ANS ellipsoid origin from geocenter, computed from satellite orbits that assume a geocentric Earth), and matches observation to ~cm precision. The dome claims this shift 'reveals hidden topology' but has no dome-derived prediction for the direction or magnitude of the shift. The globe predicts ~200 m northeast; the dome predicts nothing specific; observation is ~200 m northeast. We currently note this qualitatively; making it quantitative ('globe predicts to cm, dome predicts 0 digits') is more damaging.
sources: ICSM GDA technical documentation; Featherstone et al. 2011 JGR 'Australian datum transformation'

kernel_of_truth.description: V13 is the most mathematically elaborate dome predictive attempt to date. Unlike qualitative flat-earth claims, it actually produces numerical predictions for specific inter-city distances that can be tested. The Nullarbor ~1.00 ratio observation is correct and non-trivial. The AGD66→GDA94 shift is a real ~200 m displacement. Road distances genuinely do exceed geodesics, systematically and with pattern. A dome defender can honestly say: 'at least we are trying to make quantitative predictions' — which is more than can be said for many competing frameworks.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being quantitative is the bare minimum for scientific testing, not a success. The quantitative attempt fails by 4–5 orders of magnitude against standard geodesy, and actually fails INTERNALLY: same-hemisphere error exceeds cross-equatorial error, inverting the V13 prediction's own directional claim. Trying and failing is not confirming. The AGD66→GDA94 example does the opposite of supporting the dome: the globe framework predicted and explained the shift to sub-meter precision using standard geodetic tools; the dome offers no derivation at all, just the observation that it happened.

deeper_analysis: WIN-069's current state reflects two patch waves: (i) pre-c2 polish (commit 9b643fa + 121d487, 2026-04-08) that added the Nullarbor ~1.07/1.00 distinction, the 10,000×–250,000× range, and the selection-bias paragraph; (ii) EXP-054 post-hoc standardization (commit 58dbd15, 2026-04-10) that appended ~950 chars of generic 'this is retrodiction, not prediction' boilerplate to detail_verdict_text. The first wave was targeted and healthy — it closed three of c2's four holes. The second wave is where new holes emerged: the standardized boilerplate was written for WINs where derives_from_dome=true (dome-parameter-derived numerical claims), and when grafted onto WIN-069 (tagged derives_from_dome=false), it creates the tag↔text contradiction flagged as hole #1. The same kind of mismatch is likely present in other WINs tagged derives_from_dome=false that also received the EXP-054 template — a broader audit is warranted (log: recommend an analyst sweep of all 62 EXP-054-patched WINs to check tag↔boilerplate consistency). Separately, the '10,000× vs. mm-GNSS' numerical mismatch (hole #2) has been sitting in the text since 2026-04-08 and survived c2; it survives now because c2 flagged it only as imprecise and recommended 'order of magnitude', but the subsequent patch kept the specific number while expanding the range, amplifying rather than fixing the issue. The 'Misleading' verdict remains correct — nothing here flips to Refuted or Self-Contradicted — but the internal tag/text consistency and the headline quantitative claim need repair before a competent critic reads this in full.

citations_verified: Wikipedia: Geocentric Datum of Australia 1994 — verified in c2, still resolves, still describes AGD66→GDA94 accurately.
USQ pressbooks: '2-2 Australian Datums' — verified in c2, still resolves, still describes AGD66 as non-geocentric by design with Johnston Origin.
dome_site link https://john09289.github.io/predictions in detail_verdict_text — boilerplate internal reference; domain resolves.
citations_unchecked: Internal site anchor #posthoc-standard (Part 3.1b reference) — depends on built HTML, not validated from JSON alone.
Huber & Rust (2016), Giacomin & Levinson (2015) — cited in stronger_arguments as RECOMMENDED additions; not currently in text.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: UNRESOLVED since c2: WIN-057 has derives_from_dome=true for V13 topology (structural derivation view), WIN-069 has derives_from_dome=false for V13 applied to 12 roads (calibration view). c2 flagged this as 'defensible but debatable'; the EXP-054 detail_verdict_text boilerplate on WIN-069 now says the numerical value IS derived from dome parameters, contradicting the false tag and re-opening the inconsistency. Recommendation: flip WIN-069.derives_from_dome to true (see hole #1 recommendation). WIN-027's (relabels_standard=true, derives_from_dome=false) combination remains OK — the quadratic in WIN-027 really is a relabel of the known road-excess pattern without a dome-geometry derivation.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A capable dome defender steelman: 'Your review commits three sins you loudly condemn. (1) You assert that terrain-based models outperform V13 but cite none — so by your own Principle of Discriminatory Power you are comparing our specific 6.2% RMSE against an imagined alternative. (2) Your comparison ratios are numerically inconsistent: "10,000× vs. millimeter-level GNSS" is off by 4–5 orders of magnitude — millimeter-scale accuracy on 4,000 km is 1 part in 4×10⁹, so the ratio is ~10⁸, not 10⁴. If your central headline number is sloppy by that much, why should a reader trust the finding? (3) Your own verdict text says our numerical value "is one the dome framework derives from its own geometric or symbolic parameters," while your own tagged code_analysis says we do not derive from dome geometry. Which is it? At least V13 attempts a quantitative prediction with one internally consistent value. Your review attacks V13 with two values that conflict with each other.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: This defense is unfortunately well-aimed because all three sub-arguments are real and verifiable by any second AI reading our text. Fixes, in priority order: (a) resolve the derives_from_dome tag/text contradiction (hole #1) — this is the single most citable internal inconsistency; (b) fix the 10,000× comparison endpoints to a single defensible ratio or an internally-consistent range (hole #2); (c) add one citation to a terrain-based / detour-index reference so 'terrain-based models outperform' is no longer assertion-only (hole #4). Doing all three neutralizes the defense to rating 2 or lower.

claims_checked: Nullarbor central straight (~146 km) road-to-geodesic ratio ≈ 1.00
6.2% of 4,000 km transcontinental route → ~248 km error
quantitative_verification.computations: Computed: π × R_mean(6371.0088 km) = 20015.114 km — exact match to a = 20,015 km (rounded). Haversine Norseman (−32.20°, 121.80°E) → Ceduna (−32.13°, 133.68°E) = 1117.7 km great-circle; reported road ≈ 1,200 km; ratio = 1,200 / 1,117.7 = 1.074 — matches '~1.07' claim. 90-Mile Straight (Balladonia −32.35°, 123.62° to Caiguna −32.27°, 125.49°, ~1.88° longitude at 32°S) great-circle ≈ 177 km; the 146.6 km 'straight' road is a sub-segment of this corridor whose geodesic ≈ 146.6 km (ellipsoidal curvature <1 m over 146 km). Ratio consistent with 'approaches 1.00' and with the 0.4% upper bound stated in the TLDRs. 6.2% × 4,000 km = 248 km — matches 'errors measured in hundreds of kilometers'. 6.2% / 2.5×10⁻⁷ (1 m on 4,000 km = sub-meter distance accuracy) = 2.48×10⁵ ≈ 250,000× — matches the 250,000× endpoint of our stated range, but this endpoint is labelled 'sub-mm VLBI geodesy' (which is ~1 m / ~10⁶ m = 10⁻⁹, yielding a much larger ratio). 6.2% / 2.5×10⁻¹⁰ (mm on 4,000 km) = 2.48×10⁸ ≈ 2.5×10⁸× — NOT 10,000× as our text claims for the mm-GNSS endpoint. The text's lower endpoint (10,000×) corresponds to ~meter-scale positioning (~1 m absolute on a single point, comparable to consumer GPS), not millimeter-level geodetic GNSS.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: The '10,000× vs. millimeter-level GNSS positioning' framing is numerically wrong by 4 orders of magnitude. The actual ratio against millimeter-level geodetic GNSS is ~10⁸. Our '250,000× vs. sub-mm VLBI geodesy' endpoint matches a sub-meter (1 m) distance comparator, not a sub-mm VLBI baseline comparator (which would give ~10⁹×). Both endpoints are labelled with comparators roughly 4–5 orders of magnitude more precise than the numbers actually describe. See hole #2 for the remediation.
